name: Planet Labs PBC

profile.description: Planet Labs PBC operates the world's largest commercial Earth observation satellite constellation with 200+ satellites, providing daily global imaging data to governments, agriculture, forestry, defense, and financial intelligence customers. A certified Public Benefit Corporation, Planet achieved its first full-year adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow profitability in FY2026, and delivered record Q1 FY2027 revenue of $94.2M (up 42% YoY). The company is pivoting from a satellite imaging operator to an AI-powered data-intelligence platform, with partnerships including Anthropic and Google.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Planet pioneered the small-satellite constellation model for daily global imaging and has successfully pivoted from satellite hardware operator to a data-intelligence platform provider, achieving first full-year EBITDA and free cash flow profitability ($52.9M FCF) in FY2026 — a significant business model transformation that unlocked new revenue streams [1][2]. AI Deployment (2/2): Planet Insights Platform, launched in 2024 and expanded through 2025-2026, uses machine learning to automatically detect objects and environmental changes; AI-powered analytics products (crop health monitoring, deforestation detection, maritime tracking) are shipping to enterprise customers with measurable value; partnerships with Anthropic and Google for AI integration, including Project Suncatcher exploring space-based AI data centers [2][5]. Market Share (2/2): Planet operates the world's largest commercial Earth observation constellation — undisputed category leader in daily global revisit imaging, with no competitor matching the combination of coverage, cadence, and archive depth [1][2]. Pipeline (2/2): FY2027 guidance raised to $425-441M after record Q1 FY2027 revenue of $94.2M (+42% YoY); backlog of $906M (+72% YoY); RPO of $816M (+81% YoY); Defense & Intelligence revenue growing 65%+ YoY [3][4]. R&D Intensity (1/1): Planet's R&D is embedded in ongoing constellation development, AI analytics platform engineering, and next-generation satellite design; the company's entire business model is built on continuous technical innovation [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Planet's mission — 'using space to help life on Earth' — is transformative, service-oriented, and validated by 13 years of consistent execution building the world's largest commercial Earth imaging constellation. The company's PBC (Public Benefit Corporation) legal structure reinforces that the mission is not just marketing — it is legally embedded in governance. Strategic decisions (humanitarian data programs, climate monitoring partnerships, data access for NGOs) clearly trace to this purpose [5][6]. However, at 13 years old, the mission has not yet been tested through multi-decade leadership transitions or sustained market crises at scale. Community Impact (1/2): Planet provides data to disaster response organizations, climate researchers, and NGOs at reduced rates; imagery has been used in humanitarian monitoring of deforestation, conflict zones, and natural disaster response [5][6]. Employee Retention (0/2): Planet's 800-person team has remained under consistent founder-led leadership through SPAC-era turbulence and profitability milestones, but no specific retention data, voluntary turnover rates, or average tenure figures are publicly available. Insider Buying (0/2): Net insider selling of approximately $29M over the last 12 months; CEO Will Marshall filed intent to sell 200,000 shares ($7.2M) in April 2026; director Kristen Robinson sold $1.3M in shares in January 2026 [7]. While insider selling at a company that recently appreciated 10x in a year is not unusual, the pattern is clearly net-selling, not net-buying.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Growth sleeve stock. Liquidity check: $34.17 x 14,527,975 avg daily volume = $496.4M, well above $1M threshold. Mechanically scored on available metrics. D/E: 110.0% vs target 70%, floor 300% -> score 0.83 (1x). ROE: -84.0% vs target 15%, floor 0% -> score 0.00 (2x). Revenue Growth YoY: 42.1% vs target 10%, floor -20% -> score 1.00 (1x). Revenue Growth 5yr CAGR: ~20% vs target 10%, floor -5% -> score 1.00 (2x). Forward P/E: 10,261 vs target 20, floor 60 -> score 0.00 (1x). Profit Margin: -111.2% vs target 20%, floor 0% -> score 0.00 (1x). Forward PEG: null (excluded — meaningless with negative earnings). Weighted sum: (1x0.83) + (2x0.00) + (1x1.00) + (2x1.00) + (1x0.00) + (1x0.00) = 3.83 / 8.0 available weights = 0.479 x 10 = 4.8, rounded to 5 [8]. The improvement from the prior screen reflects the updated stock price ($34.17 vs $26.97), higher revenue growth (42.1% vs 32.6%), and first full-year FCF profitability ($52.9M). The structural weakness remains: Planet is GAAP unprofitable with -111.2% profit margin and negative ROE, dragging Infrastructure despite exceptional top-line growth.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (9/9): Planet Labs is structurally immune to AI disruption — it IS the data source that AI Earth observation models are trained on. The physical satellite constellation (200+ satellites imaging the entire Earth daily), the proprietary multi-year historical archive, and the orbital infrastructure cannot be replicated by software or AI. AI cannot substitute for satellites in orbit. Moreover, AI is a demand driver: the more AI Earth observation models proliferate, the more they need Planet's data. Defense & Intelligence customers (65%+ YoY growth) require verified, authoritative satellite imagery that AI-generated synthetic data cannot replace [1][4]. Analysts identify no AI substitution risk; the consensus is that AI amplifies Planet's competitive moat rather than threatening it [5]. AI Opportunity (1/1): Planet generates quantifiable revenue from AI-powered products through the Planet Insights Platform, which uses machine learning to detect objects, monitor crop health, track deforestation, and identify maritime vessels. The company has announced partnerships with Anthropic for AI integration and Google for Project Suncatcher (space-based AI data centers). CEO Will Marshall has stated that AI will unlock civil and commercial markets by enabling customers to build bespoke applications on Planet's data, representing a material and growing share of revenue [2][4].

infinite_game.summary: Planet Labs demonstrates strong infinite game characteristics — a genuine Just Cause embedded in its PBC legal structure, a transformative business model pivot from satellite operator to data platform, and a competitive posture oriented toward self-improvement rather than rival destruction. The company maintained its mission through years of unprofitability, SPAC-era volatility, and competitive pressure. However, at 13 years old under a single founder-CEO, the 'endurance' test has not yet been fully administered — the real test of infinite thinking comes when the company faces its first true crisis of purpose under pressure.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3 points): Planet's cause — 'using space to help life on Earth' — meets Sinek's criteria: it is resilient (survives beyond any single product), inclusive (invites partners and customers to use data for their own purposes), service-oriented (explicitly designed to benefit others), idealistic (a fully indexed, continuously monitored planet does not yet exist), and big (can never be fully achieved). The PBC corporate structure legally embeds this purpose into governance — a structural commitment most companies lack [5][6]. The cause has been validated by 13 years of consistent execution through funding crunches, SPAC-era volatility, and competitive threats. However, the company has not yet undergone a true existential test of this purpose — a deep recession, a hostile takeover attempt, or a leadership transition — that would elevate it to the highest tier. Sinek would approve of the cause but want to see it survive a true crucible.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2 points): Planet's 800-person team has operated under consistent founder-led leadership (Will Marshall as CEO since founding), which provides organizational stability. The company navigated from startup through SPAC listing to profitability without disruptive leadership changes. Strategic executive additions in product, marketing, and people operations through 2025-2026 suggest deliberate organizational development [2]. However, at 800 employees, the company is small enough that trust dynamics are largely driven by the founder's personal relationships, which is different from demonstrating scalable trust infrastructure. No specific retention data, employee satisfaction metrics, or cultural evidence beyond leadership consistency is available.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1 point): Planet explicitly acknowledges Maxar (high-resolution defense imaging), Capella Space and Umbra (SAR all-weather imaging), BlackSky (real-time intelligence), and Airbus Defence (government contracts) as competitors whose distinct capabilities push Planet to improve its own revisit rate, analytics, and multi-spectral offerings [1][5]. Management's competitive language focuses on improving Planet's unique daily global coverage and AI analytics rather than 'beating' or 'destroying' rivals. The Q1 FY2027 earnings call framed competitive dynamics in terms of market expansion rather than zero-sum share capture [4].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2 points): Planet's pivot from satellite hardware manufacturer/operator to AI-powered data-intelligence platform is a genuine existential flexibility event. The company recognized that the long-term value resides in the analytics layer built on its unique daily global imagery archive, not in the satellites themselves. This required fundamentally rethinking the business — from selling satellite passes and imagery to selling insights, monitoring, and intelligence [1][2]. The pivot was driven by the Just Cause (making Earth data useful for life on Earth requires intelligence, not just images), not by financial desperation. Project Suncatcher with Google represents a further pivot into space-based AI infrastructure. Sinek would recognize this as a company willing to reinvent its business model in service of its purpose.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2 points): Planet maintained investment in satellite launches, constellation replenishment, and data infrastructure through years of significant operating losses to reach FCF profitability ($52.9M) in FY2026 — this required sustained courage as a public company facing investor pressure for near-term results [1]. The decision to pursue a PBC structure (legally committing to stakeholder considerations beyond shareholder returns) was a meaningful courageous choice. However, Planet has not yet made a truly asymmetric, painful bet — the kind Sinek looks for where a company walks away from profitable revenue or makes a decision that costs real money in service of the cause. The profitability milestone validates the original strategy rather than demonstrating a new transformative sacrifice.

investment_thesis: Planet Labs delivered a breakout Q1 FY2027 with record revenue of $94.2M (+42% YoY), raised full-year guidance to $425-441M, and demonstrated accelerating momentum across all business lines — Defense & Intelligence revenue surging 65%+ YoY, commercial growing 20%+, and backlog holding at $906M (+72% YoY) [3][4]. The company achieved Rule of 40 status for the third consecutive quarter with 56% non-GAAP gross margin. With $731M in cash (+223% YoY), first full-year FCF profitability ($52.9M in FY2026), and an AI-powered data-intelligence platform built on the world's only daily global imagery archive, Planet is transitioning from a speculative space company to a high-growth data franchise. The Inspire Impact Score of +39 (ALIGNED) with zero negative attributions makes this a rare combination of mission-driven growth and faith alignment. At $34.17 per share ($10.1B market cap), the stock has appreciated significantly but backlog coverage provides 2+ years of revenue visibility [1][3][4].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on profitability and valuation. Planet's GAAP profit margin of -111.2% and ROE of -84% reflect a company that is still deeply unprofitable on a GAAP basis — the $52.9M FCF profitability in FY2026 was achieved through working capital timing and non-cash adjustments, not operating income [1][8]. The forward P/E of 10,261 is essentially meaningless, indicating the market is pricing a future that does not yet exist in earnings. At $10.1B market cap on ~$425M expected revenue, PL trades at 24x revenue — a premium that requires sustained 40%+ growth and a credible path to GAAP profitability. Net insider selling of $29M in the last 12 months, including CEO Will Marshall's $7.2M planned sale, suggests that management is taking chips off the table after a 10x stock appreciation [7]. Competition from well-capitalized aerospace primes (Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman) committing to satellite constellations could overwhelm Planet's position within 3-5 years given their resource advantages, and the fragmented satellite data market offers no structural protection against deep-pocketed new entrants [5].
key_catalysts: FY2027 revenue exceeding $441M (high end of raised guidance) would confirm sustained 40%+ growth and could drive re-rating toward software/data platform multiples as the AI analytics revenue mix increases

First quarterly GAAP profitability — likely achievable by Q3-Q4 FY2027 given the FCF trajectory and gross margin expansion to 56% — would remove the last institutional investor objection and unlock index inclusion eligibility

New multi-year government contract wins exceeding $100M annually (US Space Force, NATO allies, Five Eyes intelligence partners) would validate Planet's transition from tactical imagery supplier to strategic intelligence infrastructure
key_risks: GAAP profitability remains elusive: -111.2% profit margin and -84% ROE mean the company burns cash on an operating basis despite positive FCF, and any working capital reversal or satellite impairment could eliminate the FCF cushion

Valuation fragility at 24x revenue: the $10.1B market cap requires sustained 40%+ growth with margin expansion — any guidance miss or growth deceleration could trigger severe multiple compression given the stock's 10x appreciation over the past year

Aerospace prime competition: if Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, or a major tech company (Amazon's Kuiper, SpaceX's Starshield) commits to daily global imaging constellations, their resource advantages in government relationships, launch access, and capital could erode Planet's category leadership within 3-5 years
